title: Usable User Authentication on a Smartwatch using Vibration
venue: Proceedings of the 2021 ACM SIGSAC Conference on Computer and Communications Security (CCS ’21)

method.input_representation: 756 features including statistical features, and peak indices/heights from FFT, DCT, DWT, PSD, and auto-correlation
method.training_strategy: Verification model combining a multi-class classifier (Random Forest) and a binary classifier (SVM)

evaluation.context: Achieved on first-measured signals with 5 default users

evaluation_protocol: 1:1 matching (verification) using a challenge-response scheme with a random sequence of five vibration types

reported_limitations: High body fat rate (BFR) reduces distinguishability of vibration response signals
Requires vibration enrollment setup when smartwatch bands are changed

usability.usability_issues: Initial enrollment process requires significant time (approximately 9 minutes per vibration type)

key_contribution: Proposed a challenge-response authentication method using default low-frequency vibrations and low-sampling frequency sensors on commercial smartwatches
Demonstrated that physical body structure (BFR, SMR) can be used for independent smartwatch authentication
main_strength: No additional sensors or hardware required
High usability as it requires no active user interaction
main_weakness: Sensitivity to user physical composition (specifically high Body Fat Rate)
insights.notes_for_review: The study highlights a tradeoff between Body Fat Rate and signal distinguishability.
